text:
Training a deep learning model for biomedical image segmentation is generally considered a very challenging task, because of the usually limited amount of annotated training data available. In this work we introduce a new architecture for a convolutional neural network, called U-Net, and a training strategy suited for this architecture and for the constrained amount of training data typically available in biomedical image segmentation. The proposed network architecture is composed of an entrance and an exit branch, named contracting and expanding path, respectively. The contracting path exploits the properties of feature learning by the convolutional layers to capture the overall context in the input images, while the expanding path enables the network to focus on the details required to achieve high accuracy segmentations at the pixel level. In order to train the network to be robust against biological variations present in different instances of the same image modality, we use elastic deformations in combination with small batch sizes, the latter allowing for many different augmentations to be seen during training.

The network allows for end-to-end training and predicts the segmentation masks directly. Experiments on the ISBI challenge dataset for the neuronal structure segmentation from electron microscopy images demonstrate that U-Net outperforms the state-of-the-art sliding-window approaches based on convolutional networks. We also achieved the first place in the 2015 ISBI cell tracking challenge for transmitted light microscopy (phase contrast and DIC) with a large margin. The U-Net is also very fast and can predict the masks for 512x512 images in less than one second on a modern GPU. Our Caffe-based implementation along with the pre-trained models are made available to the public.
